note_data.title: Electric fields
keyPoints: **Electric field strength** is defined as force per unit positive charge: E = F/q
**Coulomb's law** states that force between two point charges is F = kQ₁Q₂/r² where k = 1/4πε₀
Electric field lines show the **direction of force** on a positive test charge and never cross each other
**Electric potential** at a point is the work done per unit positive charge in bringing charge from infinity to that point
**Potential difference** between two points is the work done per unit charge in moving charge between the points
**Capacitance** is defined as charge stored per unit potential difference: C = Q/V
Energy stored in a capacitor can be calculated using W = ½QV = ½CV² = ½Q²/C
In **uniform electric fields**, E = V/d where V is potential difference and d is separation
**Equipotential surfaces** are perpendicular to electric field lines and no work is done moving along them
Field strength at distance r from point charge Q is E = Q/4πε₀r²

definitions.definition: The force per unit positive charge experienced by a small test charge placed at that point
The work done per unit positive charge in bringing a small test charge from infinity to that point
The charge stored per unit potential difference across the capacitor
A region of space where a charged object experiences an electric force

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Learn exact definitions - include 'per unit' phrases for field strength, potential and capacitance
Show working clearly, include units, use correct formula from formula sheet

modelAnswer.question: A parallel plate capacitor has plates of area 2.5 × 10⁻³ m² separated by 1.2 mm of air. Calculate the capacitance and the charge stored when connected to a 12 V battery.

commonMistakes: Never confuse electric field strength with electric potential - field strength is force per unit charge, potential is energy per unit charge
Students often forget that electric field lines show direction of force on POSITIVE charge, not negative charge
Never say 'current flows through capacitor' - charge accumulates on plates but no current flows through dielectric
Students often use wrong units - field strength is N/C or V/m, potential is V (volts), capacitance is F (farads)
Never draw electric field lines crossing each other - they must never intersect
examinerTips: This topic appears almost every year in Paper 4 calculations
Always show clear working for capacitor calculations and include units
Learn the three energy formulas for capacitors - different questions need different forms
Field line diagrams are common - practice drawing uniform and radial fields
Remember ε₀ = 8.85 × 10⁻¹² F/m is given on formula sheet
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Parallel plate capacitor showing uniform field lines perpendicular to plates, and radial field pattern around point charge with field lines radiating outward from positive charge
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to describe the electric field pattern around a charged sphere and explain how field strength varies with distance
O/N 2022 P1 Q12 (1 mark) — multiple choice question testing understanding of electric field direction around positive and negative point charges
M/J 2022 P2 Q3b (4 marks) — students had to calculate electric field strength at a point between two charged plates and explain the motion of a charged particle
O/N 2023 P2 Q5 (5 marks) — required drawing field lines around two opposite charges and explaining why field strength is greatest where lines are closest together

workedExample.question: Two parallel plates are separated by 4.0 cm. A potential difference of 120 V is applied across the plates. Calculate the electric field strength between the plates and describe the motion of an electron placed between them. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify the formula for uniform electric field: E = V/d
Step 2: Convert distance to metres: d = 4.0 cm = 0.040 m
Step 3: Calculate field strength: E = 120 V ÷ 0.040 m = 3000 V/m or 3000 N/C
Step 4: Determine electron motion: Electric field points from positive to negative plate, so electron (negative charge) experiences force towards positive plate and accelerates in that direction
workedExample.answer: Electric field strength = 3000 V/m. The electron will accelerate towards the positive plate as it experiences a force opposite to the field direction.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct formula, 1 mark for unit conversion, 1 mark for correct calculation with units, 1 mark for describing electron motion towards positive plate

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused electric field direction with the direction of force on negative charges' — always remember field direction is defined as the direction of force on a POSITIVE test charge

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently drew field lines crossing each other or starting/ending in empty space' — field lines must never cross and always start on positive charges and end on negative charges
Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates often forgot to include units when calculating electric field strength' — always include V/m or N/C and ensure consistent unit usage throughout calculations
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw electric field line patterns showing: (1) Field lines around a single positive charge radiating outward, (2) Field lines around a single negative charge pointing inward, (3) Field lines between two opposite charges curving from positive to negative, and (4) Uniform parallel field lines between two charged plates. Include arrows showing direction, ensure lines never cross, and show that closer spacing indicates stronger field strength.
note_data.quickRecap: Electric field is the region where charged objects experience a force. Field strength E = F/q or E = V/d for uniform fields. Field lines show direction (positive to negative) and strength (spacing). Remember: field direction is defined by force on positive charges, and field strength is measured in V/m or N/C.
